text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Attending: Gynecologic Oncology
Visit Type: Established Patient - Treatment Follow-Up / End-of-Treatment Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Marguerite Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American female with FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant, c.5266dupC), status post R0 primary debulking surgery followed by adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy, now presenting for post-cycle 4 evaluation and review of completed cycles 5 and 6. Today's visit serves as the formal end-of-treatment assessment and transition-to-maintenance discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was diagnosed with bilateral FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (pT3cN1M0) after presenting with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pressure, and a 9-pound unintentional weight loss. Initial CA-125 at diagnosis was 4,872 U/mL. She underwent successful primary debulking surgery achieving R0 resection (complete gross resection) approximately 6 months ago, with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, sigmoid mesenteric implant resection, and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping. Final pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with STIC at bilateral fimbrial ends, 4/18 right pelvic nodes positive, 0/20 left pelvic nodes, 2/12 para-aortic nodes positive; all margins negative. She carries a germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant (the 5382insC Eastern European/founder mutation). Somatic tumor sequencing (Foundation One CDx) confirmed TP53 p.R175H, BRCA1 somatic LOH, RB1 deletion, CCNE1 amplification, and PIK3CA p.E545K, with an HRD score of 62 (HRD-positive).

She initiated adjuvant carboplatin AUC 6/paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV q21 days approximately 6 weeks post-operatively (after achieving ECOG PS 1 recovery). The treatment course has been notable for:

- Cycle 1: Well tolerated, no acute infusion reactions.
- Cycle 2: Developed Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral distal stocking-and-glove distribution); Grade 1 alopecia; Grade 1 nausea (managed with ondansetron/prochlorperazine PRN); Grade 1 neutropenia (ANC 1.9). Paclitaxel continued at full dose. CA-125 at cycle 2 day 1: 287 U/mL (declining from post-operative nadir of 843 U/mL).
- Cycle 3: Interim restaging CT (abdomen/pelvis/chest with contrast) showed complete resolution of ascites, no peritoneal implants, no omental nodularity, no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes — complete radiographic response at interim imaging.
- Cycle 4: Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction to carboplatin (flushing, urticaria, chest tightness, pruritus) occurring approximately 12 minutes into the carboplatin infusion. Infusion halted; managed with diphenhydramine, hydrocortisone, and prepared but not administered epinephrine. Symptoms resolved within 20 minutes. Successful re-challenge at slower infusion rate (2-hour infusion) with augmented premedication (cetirizine added, dexamethasone increased to 40 mg IV). Full carboplatin dose delivered via extended desensitization at cycle 4. Allergist/immunology consulted.
- Cycles 5 and 6: Completed using a formal 12-step carboplatin desensitization protocol per the allergy/immunology team, with no further hypersensitivity events. Both cycles delivered at full planned doses (carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2).

CA-125 trend:
  - Diagnosis: 4,872 U/mL
  - Post-operative (pre-chemotherapy): 843 U/mL
  - Cycle 2 day 1: 287 U/mL
  - Cycle 4: 48 U/mL
  - End of treatment (post cycle 6): 11 U/mL (NORMALIZED; institution ULN <35 U/mL)

HE4 end of treatment: 58 pmol/L (normalized, within institutional normal range).

Today's visit is focused on the post-chemotherapy assessment, review of residual toxicities, confirmation of complete response, and planning for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy initiation given her BRCA1-mutated status.

Ms. Okonkwo reports that she has been feeling progressively better over the past 3-4 weeks since completing her last chemotherapy cycle. She returns to the office today in good spirits. She has no new complaints of abdominal pain, distension, early satiety, or pelvic pressure. She denies nausea, vomiting, or significant fatigue at this visit. She notes that the tingling in her hands and feet, which was most pronounced after cycles 3 and 4, has improved considerably and is now only a barely perceptible "occasional faint tingling" in the tips of her toes bilaterally. She reports that her hair, which was completely lost (Grade 2 alopecia) during cycles 2 through 4, is beginning to show fine regrowth. She is relieved that her CA-125 has normalized. She continues to work part-time from home as a high school principal and is looking forward to returning to full duties.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fever, no chills. Weight reported as stable (she believes she has regained approximately 4 of the 9 pounds she lost prior to diagnosis). Fatigue: Grade 1, markedly improved compared to mid-chemotherapy nadir.
Neurologic: Peripheral neuropathy — bilateral distal lower extremity tingling, Grade 1, stable and improving since paclitaxel completion. No new weakness, no gait disturbance, no upper extremity functional limitations.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal distension. No early satiety. Normal bowel function; she reports she had intermittent constipation during chemotherapy that has now resolved. No nausea at present (last episode of nausea was during cycle 5). No rectal bleeding.
Gynecologic: She is surgically menopausal. Reports mild hot flashes (vasomotor symptoms), approximately 5-6 episodes per day, manageable. Vaginal dryness noted, not using any local therapy yet.
Dermatologic: Alopecia — hair regrowth beginning, fine vellus hair visible over scalp. Nail changes: Mild onycholysis of several fingernails (Grade 1), stable.
Musculoskeletal: Mild arthralgias, particularly in the mornings — consistent with paclitaxel-associated arthralgia/myalgia, also potentially contributed to by surgical menopause. Improving week-over-week.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no dyspnea, no palpitations. Home blood pressures reported as 122-130/78-84 mmHg on amlodipine 5 mg daily — well controlled.
Psychosocial: She reports positive mood and optimism now that treatment is completed. Continues meeting with oncology social work monthly. Her two children have been informed of the germline BRCA1 finding; the older child (age 22) is pursuing genetic counseling/testing and the younger child (age 19) is considering testing.

---

MEDICATIONS (current at this visit):
1. Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (hypertension)
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
3. Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN nausea
4. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea
5. Acetyl-L-carnitine 500 mg PO BID (initiated for neuropathy support, patient preference)
6. Diphenhydramine (as part of prior premedication regimen; no longer ongoing)
7. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI prophylaxis during dexamethasone premedication — patient continues PRN for reflux)
8. Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES:
- Carboplatin: Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction (urticaria, flushing, chest tightness) — managed with desensitization protocol; carboplatin not contraindicated but requires desensitization for future courses.
- No other known drug allergies.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Temperature 37.0°C, Blood Pressure 126/80 mmHg, Heart Rate 74 bpm (regular), Respiratory Rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 99% on room air, Weight 64.1 kg (BSA ~1.74 m2), Height 163 cm.
ECOG Performance Status: 0 (fully active, able to carry on all pre-disease activities without restriction).

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Affect bright and engaged. Fine scalp hair regrowth visible.
Head/Neck: Atraumatic, normocephalic. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpable. Thyroid not enlarged.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion at the lung bases (no pleural effusion clinically).
Abdominal: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healing midline laparotomy scar without erythema or induration. No palpable organomegaly. No ascites — no shifting dullness, negative fluid wave. No palpable masses.
Pelvic/Rectal (limited exam, patient post-total hysterectomy/BSO): Vaginal cuff appears well-healed. No vaginal cuff mass palpable. No pelvic tenderness on examination.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Mild onycholysis of bilateral second and third fingernails, Grade 1. No petechiae.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Fine touch sensation mildly reduced at bilateral distal toes (Grade 1). Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Gait normal. Romberg negative. Upper extremity sensation normal.
Skin: Mild diffuse hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases (chemotherapy-associated, mild). No rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CBC (today):
  - WBC: 5.1 x10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 3.2 x10^9/L (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mildly below normal; improving trend from nadir of 10.1 g/dL during treatment; consistent with chemotherapy-associated anemia in recovery)
  - Platelets: 287 x10^9/L (normal)
  - MCV: 89 fL (normocytic)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, CO2: 25 mEq/L
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2; acceptable for niraparib initiation)
  - Glucose: 92 mg/dL
  - AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 18 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase: 68 U/L, Total Bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL (LFTs normal)
  - Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
  - Total Protein: 7.1 g/dL

TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (euthyroid on current levothyroxine dose).

Tumor Markers:
  - CA-125: 11 U/mL (NORMALIZED; below institutional ULN of 35 U/mL)
  - HE4: 58 pmol/L (normalized, within normal limits for premenopausal/postmenopausal range)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
End-of-chemotherapy restaging CT (abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest, performed after cycle 6): No evidence of disease. Post-surgical and post-treatment changes. No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental thickening. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the pelvis or retroperitoneum. No hepatic lesions. No pleural effusion. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no mediastinal adenopathy, no pleural effusion. Complete radiographic response maintained — no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent ovarian carcinoma. This imaging is consistent with our clinical and biochemical impression of complete response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. FIGO Stage IIIC HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, BRCA1-mutated (germline c.5266dupC pathogenic variant), pT3cN1M0 — STATUS POST R0 PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY AND 6 CYCLES CARBOPLATIN/PACLITAXEL ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY — COMPLETE CLINICAL, BIOCHEMICAL, AND RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE:

Ms. Okonkwo has achieved an excellent end-of-treatment response. She completed all 6 planned cycles of carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 without dose reductions for either agent (neuropathy remained at Grade 1-2, not meeting threshold for paclitaxel dose reduction). Her CA-125 has normalized to 11 U/mL, HE4 is normalized, and end-of-treatment CT imaging confirmed a complete radiographic response with no evidence of residual peritoneal, nodal, or visceral disease. Her ECOG performance status has improved to 0. This is a gratifying response in a patient with a challenging diagnosis of stage IIIC HGSOC.

Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, HRD-positive tumor (HRD score 62 by Foundation One CDx), R0 resection, and complete biochemical response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, she is an ideal candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy, which is the standard of care supported by the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial (niraparib), SOLO-1 trial (olaparib in BRCA-mutated patients), and current NCCN guidelines (Category 1 recommendation).

Maintenance therapy plan: NIRAPARIB (Zejula) — will be initiated at an individualized starting dose of 200 mg PO once daily, given her body weight of 64 kg (below 77 kg threshold); this individualized starting dose strategy per the FDA label and PRIMA trial approach reduces the risk of hematologic toxicity compared with the 300 mg starting dose. However, I note that her weight of 64 kg is under the 77 kg threshold and her platelets today are 287,000/mcL (above the 150,000/mcL threshold), so per current label the individualized 200 mg dose is appropriate. I will also note for the team that olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID maintenance (per SOLO-1) is an equally acceptable option in BRCA1/2-mutated patients and was discussed with the patient; she was informed of both options. After discussion of once-daily versus twice-daily dosing and toxicity profiles, the patient prefers niraparib for the once-daily convenience. Plan confirmed: niraparib 200 mg PO once daily, taken at bedtime (to minimize nausea awareness). Ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes before niraparib dosing recommended for nausea prophylaxis.

Duration: 3 years (36 months from initiation) per PRIMA protocol duration, barring progression or intolerable toxicity.

Pre-maintenance baseline labs obtained today (CBC, CMP) — all appropriate for initiation. Will recheck CBC at 4 weeks (hematologic toxicity, particularly thrombocytopenia and anemia, is the primary concern with niraparib and requires close early monitoring). Will provide patient with written instructions to contact clinic immediately for signs of bleeding, unusual bruising, or profound fatigue.

Counseled patient extensively on potential niraparib toxicities:
  - Thrombocytopenia: Most common grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity; occurs most frequently in first 3 months; CBC monitoring schedule provided
  - Anemia: Mild-moderate expected; may require dose hold or dose reduction if symptomatic or grade 3-4
  - Nausea: Common (>20%), typically manageable; bedtime dosing and ondansetron prophylaxis recommended
  - Fatigue: Common, usually manageable
  - Hypertension: Monitor blood pressure given pre-existing hypertension on amlodipine; advise home blood pressure log
  - Rare but serious: Myelodysplastic syndrome/AML (<1%); patient counseled about persistent cytopenias and need for hematology evaluation if CBC does not normalize after hold
  - No hair loss expected with niraparib (welcome news for this patient)
  - Patient verbalized understanding of all discussed toxicities and precautions

Niraparib prescription sent to specialty pharmacy. Patient will be contacted by specialty pharmacy for insurance authorization and delivery within 5-7 business days. Office nurse will provide patient education materials and written instructions.

2. CARBOPLATIN HYPERSENSITIVITY:
Managed successfully with 12-step desensitization protocol for cycles 5 and 6. No further carboplatin required at this time. Allergy/immunology documentation on file. Should carboplatin re-treatment ever be indicated (e.g., in the event of platinum-sensitive recurrence), desensitization protocol will be required and allergy/immunology re-consultation recommended.

3. PACLITAXEL-ASSOCIATED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY:
Currently Grade 1 (mild, barely perceptible distal lower extremity tingling in the tips of the toes). Significantly improved from Grade 2 nadir during treatment. Paclitaxel has been completed; neuropathy is expected to continue improving over the next 3-6 months. The patient is aware that full resolution may take time and is not guaranteed. Acetyl-L-carnitine continued per patient preference; evidence for this is limited but risk of harm is minimal. Will monitor at each visit. No referral to neurology required at this time. If neuropathy worsens (unexpected given completion of paclitaxel), will reassess.

4. ALOPECIA:
Grade 2 alopecia during active treatment, now with visible hair regrowth. Expected to fully resolve within 3-6 months post-chemotherapy. Patient reassured. Referral to scalp cooling specialist not applicable at this point (retroactive), but patient is aware of options for future if re-treatment ever occurs.

5. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE SYMPTOMS:
Patient is experiencing hot flashes (5-6/day) and vaginal dryness following surgical menopause (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy). Hormone replacement therapy is generally considered contraindicated or used with caution in gynecologic cancers, though emerging data for HGSOC (unlike hormone-receptor positive tumors) is somewhat more nuanced. Given patient's young age (47) and quality of life concerns, this will be discussed in more detail at her next visit — I will review the literature and may consider referral to menopause specialists. In the interim:
  - For hot flashes: Discussion of non-hormonal options. Will consider escitalopram, venlafaxine, or gabapentin. Patient not currently on any treatment for vasomotor symptoms; will discuss at next visit.
  - For vaginal dryness: Recommend OTC vaginal lubricant/moisturizer (Replens or similar). Local vaginal estrogen (low-dose) has minimal systemic absorption and could be considered — will revisit with the patient.

6. HYPERTENSION:
Well-controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Blood pressure today 126/80 mmHg. Will monitor more closely once niraparib is initiated given the known association of PARP inhibitors (particularly niraparib) with hypertension as a potential adverse effect. Patient instructed to check home blood pressures twice weekly and report any readings >140/90 mmHg. Will communicate with PCP regarding monitoring.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Euthyroid on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (TSH 2.1 mIU/L). Continue current dose. Recheck TSH at 6-month follow-up.

8. FAMILY/GERMLINE BRCA1 MANAGEMENT:
The patient's germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC status was disclosed to her family. Her two children have been informed. The older child (age 22) is pursuing genetic counseling and testing. The younger child (age 19) is considering testing — patient counseled again on the importance of genetic counseling for unaffected carriers for risk-reduction strategies (enhanced surveillance and RRSO at appropriate age for any female child found to be BRCA1 positive). Genetic counseling program has been notified; family genetic management ongoing.

9. FOLLOW-UP:
  - Return to clinic in 4 weeks (to check CBC on niraparib — critical early monitoring window)
  - After initial CBC check, schedule follow-up every 4 weeks for CBC monitoring for the first 3 months on niraparib per label recommendation
  - CA-125 and clinical assessment every 3 months
  - Surveillance imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis/chest) at approximately 6 months into maintenance, then annually or as clinically indicated
  - Psychosocial support: Ongoing oncology social work; support group referral offered
  - DXA bone density scan to be ordered given surgical menopause and risk of accelerated bone loss; initiate calcium 1200 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily supplementation
  - PCP communication letter to be sent today regarding: treatment completion, niraparib initiation, blood pressure monitoring requests, and hypothyroidism/levothyroxine management

Time spent with patient: 55 minutes (>50% of time spent in counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed, Gynecologic Oncology Attending.
